morgan-kim:
Morgan Kim's poems come from a specific set of coordinates: the black loam of Canadian prairies, the red clay of South African townships, the highland earth of Ethiopia. Four collections. A farmer. A wanderer. A life measured in soils.

The work asks what it means to belong to multiple landscapes at once — and finds no clean answer. Only the poems themselves, which hold the question open.
sky-novak:
Sky Novak's work moves through Istanbul's winding streets, across hemispheres, through the spaces between departures. Haiku. Migrant blooms. Waters left behind. A life measured in crossings rather than arrivals.

The work asks what remains visible in moments of transition — what the eye catches in a borrowed city, what language carries across borders. The question stays open. The haiku hold it there, in the gap between one place and the next.
sam-brown:
Sam Brown's work moves between Sydney's worn harbour stones and Tokyo rooftops, through Miami's Art Deco beaches and the hidden corners of cities half-remembered. Kintsugi. Salt. The particular weight of air before rain. A body that learns things before the mind has words for them.

The work asks what accumulates when a life is lived across latitudes — not as loss, not as gain, but as the warmth stone holds long after the sun has moved. No clean accounting. Only the ordinary moment, held up to the light until it shows its fracture lines.
robin-andersson:
Robin Andersson's work moves through Istanbul's worn stones, seven coastlines, the light that refuses to stay in one place. Haiku caught between languages. A body measured in water rather than years. The Bosphorus in childhood; the sea again at the end.

The work asks what persists when light travels across decades and continents — whether the same brightness that fell on crumbling streets can mean the same thing from a different window. No resolution offered. Only the moment held still long enough to notice it.
sky-singh:
Sky Singh's work moves between hemispheres and seasons, accumulating what the body knows before the mind catches up — the slant of afternoon light that means something different on each continent, the weight of air before rain, the way stone holds warmth. Eight decades. Multiple cities. A life kept between ledgers and the silences ledgers cannot hold. Melbourne, now. The kigo as a kind of reckoning.

The work asks what it means to carry seasonal knowledge across latitudes — when the body's calendar no longer matches the sky overhead. Salt between places. Thresholds that open onto the wrong time of year. The question stays open. Only the light, arriving at its particular angle, insisting on an answer.
lou-bernard:
Lou Bernard's work moves through city streets and crossing points: wet pavement under lamplight, café chatter, the métro's distant hum. A self translated across cities, languages, names. What is lost in the crossing. What arrives unexpectedly in its place.

The work asks what remains when a fixed self dissolves into transit and sound — and holds that question in the ordinary details, the shadows on pavement, the measures between one version of a life and another.
arjun-desai:
Arjun Desai's work comes from four cities and the residue between them: Mumbai, Miami, Calgary, Johannesburg. Grease under the fingernails. The specific silence of a garage at six in the morning. A body that has stopped asking which place it belongs to, and started recording what the hands absorb instead.

The work holds a question about what cities do to a body over time — not through the eyes but through the work itself, the engine, the material. It finds no clean answer. Only the forms, which keep shifting, keep trying to hold the weight of it.
parker-novak:
Parker Novak's work moves through humidity and cobblestone and color — Singapore's monsoon skies, Vienna's curling ironwork, Santiago's crowded avenues. Moments collected the way light collects in broken glass: not whole, but present. Fractured and still luminous.

The work asks what ordinary life holds when you stop moving past it — what a rain-soaked street or a courtyard at dusk actually contains. No resolution offered. Only the looking itself, kept open.
robin-novak:
Robin Novak's work comes from specific coordinates: the ledger columns of exchanged hours, the particular slant of childhood streets, the thin air between altitudes. Miami. The Andes. A life measured in what came in, what went out, what remains. Working-class hours. Bodies that remember elevation.

The work asks what it means to keep your own record — against the records others keep for you. What is owed, what is lost, what the body carries from one altitude to the next. No clean answer. Only the ledger itself, held open.
morgan-durand:
Morgan Durand's work moves through the particular textures of a life lived between cities: the gray mornings of Paris, rain against stone banks, the slow accumulation of doorways and thresholds. Born in the early years of the last century. A life that passed through Johannesburg. Hands that learned and forgot and learned again.

The work asks what can be found in the unhurried moment — the lapping water, the rustling leaf, the distant laughter — when a life refuses to arrange itself into narrative. No clean story is offered. Only the thresholds themselves, held open.
harper-martin:
Harper Martin's work begins in the body — not the body as metaphor, but the body as archive. The wrist over the kettle. The shoulder's angle. What the mind revises, the body files without consent. Four collections. Portland as base, but the coordinates are interior: trauma as geography, sensation as record, the self as a landscape that keeps its own latitude regardless of what story gets told over it.

The work asks what survives when memory is unreliable but sensation is not — and whether that distinction offers shelter or only a different kind of exposure. The threshold holds. The smoke stays between.
sol-gomez:
Sol Gómez's work moves between specific coordinates: the sunbeams of Madrid's streets, California's golden skies, a river bend remembered from some unnamed elsewhere. Jasmine. Twilight. The small, exact moment before it dissolves. Three collections of haiku built from the space where self and surroundings lose their edges.

The work asks what remains when a moment passes — whether catching it in language preserves anything at all, or only marks the loss. No resolution offered. Only the next moment, and the next, held briefly in place.
cameron-andersson:
Cameron Andersson's work moves between the midnight sun's stretched light and the winds crossing Africa's savannas, Europe's misty shores, Iceland's glaciers. Whispers. Windows. The ordinary moment held long enough to become something else.

The work asks what gets carried on those winds — what the sun shares when no one is watching, what threads connect a pen in hand to a shimmering glacier to a red-dust plain. No resolution offered. Only the next quiet moment, and the attention it deserves.
sora-nakamura:
Sora Nakamura moves through misty Japanese mornings, New Zealand's coastlines, China's ancient streets — finding something to stop at in each one. Fingerprints on glass. The particular angle of dawn over a mountain, over an ocean. Two hundred and fifty haiku and still counting.

The work asks what a fleeting moment owes us, if anything — whether the light creeping over water is enough, or whether noticing it is the thing itself. No resolution. Only the next crossing, the next threshold, the shadows that arrive just before the day does.
harper-brown:
Harper Brown's work moves between coastlines and city streets, between New Zealand's rugged edges and the rhythms of Boston. Red earth underfoot. Laboratory silence at dawn. A constellation of specific places that refuse to resolve into a single home.

The work asks what accumulates in ordinary moments — the light on water, the weight of familiar ground, the hush of a room before the day begins. Not grand crossings, but the small residue of each place on a life. The question stays open.
xia-zhao:
Xia Zhao has moved through Shanghai, the Hudson, Norwegian mountains, and the Ōta River delta — that same delta where the river ran with the dead in August 1945. Eight decades of arriving. Haiku as a travel companion through cities and seasons. The silences between places as a language of their own.

The work asks what remains when impermanence is the only constant — what a syllable can hold, what a river remembers, what survives the crossing from one shore to another. No clean answer. Only the quiet intimacy of the form itself, keeping the question open.
riley-chen:
Riley Chen's work moves between the Ethiopian breeze and Philadelphia snow, between abaya fabric and storm drain runoff, between haiku and the archaeology of bottle caps in river mud. The coordinates shift — Addis to the Schuylkill, the continent-crossing past to the street dog carrying someone's lost shoe — but the attention stays fixed on what a single moment holds.

The work asks what gets carried and what gets lost when life moves across those distances. The tuk-tuk, the melting snow, the quiet hours — each one a container for something larger than itself. The question stays open in the details.
logan-jackson:
Logan Jackson's work moves between a small apartment in New Orleans and the open water beyond it — jazz drifting through windows, beignets, the slow pull of the Mississippi, Mardi Gras crowds thinning into memory. The everyday surfaces hold everything: a summer afternoon, a scent, a parade rounding a corner and gone.

What stays when the moment passes? The work sits with that question — not the grand journey but the residue of it, the way a room in New Orleans can open, without warning, onto the Rio de la Plata.
river-smith:
River Smith's work moves through the in-between: a snowflake in Zurich, a sunset over California, a gray Chicago sky, a fig left on the branch. Haiku built from fragments of seasons and the memories that cling to them. Corridors walked too fast. A table steadied by hand. The hairline fractures in ordinary moments.

The question underneath it all: what lives inside the threshold — the pause before arrival, the thing not quite finished. A snowflake, a fig, a corridor. The work holds these open, turns them slowly, and waits.
casey-walker:
Casey Walker's work moves between two worlds: the red earth of Outback Australia — spinifex grass, rust-red soil, wide open skies — and the neon hum of a Tokyo apartment. The ancient and the electric. The vast silence of desert and the soft glow of screens.

The work holds a question in the space between those distances: what remains of a landscape when you carry it far from home? No resolution offered. Only the red earth, remembered from a great remove, whispering back.
valentin-garcia:
Valentín García's work moves through specific latitudes: the cypress-lined streets of Buenos Aires, the Tennessee hills, the Arabian Gulf, the Parisian glow of streetlights at dusk. Eighty-eight years of standing beneath different skies. A childhood breeze that never quite left. A life assembled from shorelines, river mouths, and meridians.

The work asks what ordinary moments carry across that much distance — laughter in summer streets, the hum of a city at night, the particular light that falls when memory and place overlap. No resolution offered. Only the coordinates themselves, held open.
skyler-johnson:
Skyler Johnson's work moves through San Francisco mornings and a Kathmandu garden tended for nearly two decades. Light that arrives and disappears. Arrangements made not in order but the way a life accumulates — sideways, in the margins of ordinary time.

The work asks what gets held in a fleeting moment — whether the whisper left behind after the light is gone is loss or something else entirely. No clean answer. Only the moments themselves, kept open.
ming-xu:
Ming Xu's work moves through steel and stone, through foreign cities and childhood streets, through the space between a stranger's voice and something half-remembered. Haikus gathered from intersecting stories. A tapestry that keeps unraveling at the edges.

The work asks what the self becomes when the familiar and the unfamiliar share the same sky — when urban facades reflect something interior and broken. No resolution. Only the whispered conversations, the labyrinth, the light fading over cities that never quite belonged to anyone.
quinn-smith:
Quinn Smith's work begins in the hour before full light — in whispers, in the spaces where one thing becomes another. Two collections. Tapestries and mosaics. The ground between languages, between continents, between the name a child answers to and the name an adult carries.

The work asks what holds when everything is threshold — childhood dissolving into adulthood, leaving folding into arriving, home refusing a fixed definition. No resolution offered. Only the haiku, the quiet moments, the dawn rising four times over, each time from a different coordinate.
avery-johnson:
Avery Johnson writes from scattered drafts and dog-eared pages, from city streets and the wandering paths between them. *Turbulent Leaves* gathers what accumulates in motion — memories picked up and turned over like something found on a sidewalk. A fusion of places. A life measured in small discoveries.

The work asks what gets noticed when no one is looking for anything in particular — and refuses to tidy the answer. The haiku hold ordinary moments just long enough to feel the weight of them, then let go.
ryan-walsh:
Ryan Walsh's work begins in the quiet hours before dawn — in shadows on walls, in a father's voice carrying distant lands into a childhood bedroom. Four cities. Four languages. Ninety-six years of paying attention. The record of that attention is not kept in sequence but in the way memory actually moves: burning, drifting, dispersing like smoke.

The work asks what remains when the ordinary moment — the shadow, the story overheard, the smell of something burning — is held long enough to mean something. No resolution. Only the next moment, and the attention brought to it.
dakota-young:
Dakota Young's work moves between two kinds of attention: the Angeleno eye learning a new city, and the body that keeps walking anyway — through Hong Kong summers, along trails that disappear into fog, into rooftop gardens pressed between glass and heat. Concrete jungle. Unexpected green. A transplant learning to read a landscape sideways.

The work asks what it means to find nature in a city that offers only fragments of it — and whether those fragments are enough. The question stays open, somewhere between the sweltering air and the light that keeps changing, everywhere, all at once.
jamie-martinez:
Jamie Martinez's work moves through the small hours of ordinary weather: morning rain in Miami, first snow in Vermont, a Bangkok afternoon that refuses to cool. The haiku form carries these moments — cherry blossoms, ancient winds, the digital present pressing against tradition. Seasons changing. A life taken in increments.

The work asks what survives when a moment passes — whether a few syllables can hold rain, or heat, or the particular quality of a morning before anyone else is awake. No resolution offered. Only the next small thing, noticed.
jamie-rodriguez:
Jamie Rodriguez's work begins where most people aren't paying attention: the hour before cities remember themselves, the rustle of leaves underfoot, the window-side dark before the body is asked to be anything. Two collections. The maple and the street below. The fleeting moment held still long enough to become a word.

The work asks what lives inside ordinary thresholds — the seasonal, the pre-dawn, the not-yet. What gets lost when the city wakes, when the leaves settle. No resolution offered. Only the moments themselves, gathered and kept.
casey-kim:
Casey Kim's work moves through Accra's markets, Oxford's college chapels at twilight, Copenhagen's cafes, and an Auckland home crowded with photographs and trinkets. The scent of lemongrass. A lifetime collected in objects and afternoons.

The work asks what ordinary moments carry across such distances — what a trinket holds, what a scent returns to. No resolution. Only the accumulation itself, and the question of what it means to keep gathering.
cameron-brown:
Cameron Brown's work begins in particular places: Jakarta sidewalks in afternoon rain, the quiet of a morning before the steam rises. Childhood memory as a starting point. The haiku's pause. A life measured in small observances — a raindrop's path, a shift in seasonal light.

The work asks what gets lost in the ordinary moment if no one stops to look — and what gets recovered when someone does. Jakarta and its rain. Two hundred fifty small windows onto the world. The question stays open, one haiku at a time.
parker-wilson:
Parker Wilson's work moves between Brisbane heat and Stockholm cold, between jacaranda stain and snowfall, between the ancient medina and the modern street. Bare feet on hot bitumen. Frost-printed grass. The body as the first instrument of place, reading the world through skin and sole before any word is written.

The work asks what happens when a single life spans contradictory coordinates — sun-drenched and frost-bitten, rooted and in transit. No resolution is offered. Only the specific hour, the specific surface underfoot, held long enough to become something.
sacha-michel:
Sacha Michel moves through seasons the way a river moves through granite — slowly, accumulating. Fallen leaves on a forest floor. The cold coast of Maine. The spaces between stones where silence settles and takes its shape. These haiku gather what most days discard: the fragment, the encounter, the impression that passes before it can be named.

The work turns on a quiet problem — that a river does not know its name until someone else gives it one. That memory and meaning arrive late, after the wandering. These collections hold that gap open: the distance between living a moment and understanding what it was.
morgan-andersson:
Morgan Andersson's work moves through fog-wrapped Zurich mornings, monsoon-soaked streets, the space between siren calls and stillness. Espresso machines. Pedestrians mid-murmur. The ordinary caught at the exact moment before it passes.

The work asks what lives inside the pause — between urgency and quiet, between one city's grey mist and another's drenched heat. Not resolution. Only the held breath, the suspended moment, the sacred hiding inside the unremarkable.
skyler-anderson:
Skyler Anderson's work moves between a granite-cold coast in Maine and the streets of Dhaka, tracing what happens when a voice must learn to shape itself around foreign syllables. Pine sap. Unfamiliar teacups. The weight of things carried across water. Rivers that have no name until someone far from home gives them one.

The work asks what survives a crossing — what accumulates in the body, what gets left on the shore. It finds no resolution. Only the moments themselves, held together: the tremor of hands, the sound of a self still learning where it belongs.
casey-garcia:
Casey García's work moves at the pace of a nature walk through Reykjavik — attentive, unhurried, learning the names of things. Latin binomials. Leaf margins. Root systems. Fig split by noon heat. Jasmine through a crack. Cedar holding snow it will not remember. The coordinates shift — slope, limestone wall, plaster dust on sill — but the method stays the same: patient, exacting attention to what grows, what clings, what remains.

The work asks what roots actually remember, and whether the vocabulary of botany is equal to the task. Cedar and cordite. A mother's wrist. A country whose pink mouth will not close. No answer arrives. Only the names, held carefully, in tension with what they cannot contain.
parker-lewis:
Parker Lewis writes from the gap between pulse and breath — from Austin's sun-baked streets to Kolkata's monsoon-washed wards, with three other countries mapped in between. A traveling nurse. A decade of threshold moments. The stethoscope as compass, the body as territory.

The work asks what it means to tend — to others, to oneself, to the places that leave their mark. Not a clean answer anywhere in it. Only the daily observations, held close, tracing the intimate cartography of care across distances that don't shrink with time.
riley-kowalski:
Riley Kowalski works in the space between thimbles and Wehrmacht boots, between Anne Frank's chestnut tree and red tulips pushing through sidewalk cracks. The collections move through grandmother's silver, salt stains, marble steps, the turning of seasons — small objects that carry the weight of what came before.

The work asks how ordinary surfaces hold history. A queue of whispering visitors. A worn thumb-groove in metal. The question isn't answered so much as turned over, season by season, the way a thimble catches light differently depending on where you're standing when you look.

sunny-fields:
Sunny Fields works in the space between words — the chambers inside language, the silences that hold the shape of what almost got said. One observation becomes a threshold. One word opens into another, and another after that. The work moves through nested rooms, tracing what language carries inside itself without announcing it.

The question underneath is about containment and excess — what fits inside a word, and what doesn't. Whether silence is absence or its own kind of structure. No resolution offered. Only the next chamber, the next opening, the work continuing to ask what lives inside what.
simone-bruno: Simone Bruno's poems arrive from a specific territory: incorporates cultural heritage. Based in Philadelphia, USA.
test-author:
Test Author's work begins at the window — that pause before rain arrives, the hesitation between stillness and weather. Two coordinates: water gathering and silence waiting. The collections follow what accumulates slowly, what holds off, what arrives only after a long approach.

The work asks what lives in that interval — between the storm and its announcement, between a person standing at glass and whatever draws them there. No resolution. Only the slow accumulation itself, held open on the page.
timothy-henry:
Timothy Henry's work begins where the simple things refuse to stay simple. A ball. A field of grass or mud or cracked summer earth. Rules that fit on a few pages, learned in an afternoon. From these coordinates — the clean edge of a rulebook, the unpredictable surface underfoot — he writes with the kind of attention that follows a thing past where it seems to end.

The work asks what happens when something plain keeps reaching. Where the boundary of a game becomes a question about games themselves, about rules and what they cannot hold. No resolution offered. Only the next pass, the next patch of ground, and the question still moving.
number-10:
Number 10's work begins where abstraction ends: with the ball, the mud, the net swinging inward. With the body in motion and the crowd's roar and the specific weight of a goal that changes everything. Four collections. A striker. A witness. A life measured in moments of contact between foot and leather.

The work asks what joy actually is when it lives inside a game — not as symbol, not as lesson, but as the thing itself. The beautiful game resists explanation. The collections hold that resistance open, returning again and again to the real.
william-kaitel:
William Kaitel's work begins in rehearsal — in the rooms where gestures are learned, the faces studied until they become familiar, the small repeated acts of passing the salt. Three collections. A precise attention. A life measured in what gets handed down.

The work asks what it means to inherit a performance you didn't choose — the lines absorbed before you knew you were listening. No resolution is offered. Only the writing itself, which holds the question steady.
drew-miller: Drew Miller's poems arrive from a specific territory: experiments with form. Based in Toronto, Canada.
lenny-black:
Lenny Black's work moves through cul-de-sacs and culverts, vacant lots edged where construction stopped, the hours between lunch and the streetlights coming on. Bikes with loose chains. Suburban ground that is not exotic, not dramatic — just the specific weight of afternoons that stretch too long and end too fast.

The work asks what happens in those in-between hours, in places that were almost something else. Not wilderness, not city — just the familiar, slightly unfinished terrain where kids find the edges of things and wait, without knowing what for.
quinn-davies: Quinn Davies's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on environmental themes. Based in Small town Canada.
sol-vazquez: Sol Vázquez's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on urban nature. Based in Prague, Czech Republic.
seo-lim: Seo Lim's poems arrive from a specific territory: documents pandemic life. Based in Rural New Zealand.
frank-smith:
Frank Smith's work moves through ballrooms and basements, cardboard floors and parquet, church aisles on Tuesdays and kitchens at midnight. All types of dance. The nervous kind, the sweaty kind, the half-remembered kind done in private when no one is watching.

The work asks what dance actually is when you strip the beautiful away — and refuses to settle. What remains is the full range: exhausted bodies, unlikely rooms, movement that happens anyway. The question stays open on the floor.
upside-down-sactown:
Upside Down Sactown's haikus begin with Yacob — a man with a spoon, a bowl, one shoe tied, a front door he may or may not reach. Sacramento somewhere underneath it all, inverted. The coordinates here are domestic and specific: the small geometry of a morning, the ordinary objects a person moves through without asking to be observed.

The work holds one question open and does not let go of it: why would you do this. Not why write about Yacob, exactly — but why hand that task to a machine. The spoon is still there. The bowl. Yacob did not ask. The haikus exist anyway, which is either the problem or the whole point.
cruz-ruiz: Cruz Ruiz's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores spiritual themes. Based in Hong Kong, China.
yuki-kato: Yuki Kato's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on environmental themes. Based in Rome, Italy.
casey-muller:
Casey Müller's work moves between Stockholm and Paris, through the slow arithmetic of latitudes and years. Seven sections. More than two hundred haiku. A life measured not in calendars but in coordinates — the distance between languages, between bodies, between the self that was and the one that remains.

The work asks what it means to track a life as it narrows — what coordinates hold when the body begins to forget its own longitude. No resolution offered. Only the haiku themselves, which keep the latitude open, one degree at a time.
jordan-white: Jordan White's poems arrive from a specific territory: experiments with form. Based in Seoul, South Korea.
jun-ito: Jun Ito's poems arrive from a specific territory: draws inspiration from nature walks. Based in Rural India.
sol-carvalho: Sol Carvalho's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores themes of displacement. Based in Dhaka, Bangladesh.
aditya-khan: Aditya Khan's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores themes of displacement. Based in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
guadalupe-rodriguez: Guadalupe Rodríguez's poems arrive from a specific territory: writes primarily at dawn. Based in Philadelphia, USA.
peyton-anderson: Peyton Anderson's poems arrive from a specific territory: writes bilingually. Based in Brisbane, Australia.
michi-suzuki: Michi Suzuki's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on urban nature. Based in Lima, Peru.
cameron-martin: Cameron Martin's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores themes of displacement. Based in Seoul, South Korea.
coach-potato:
Coach Potato's work comes from a specific set of coordinates: the locked door, the shaped couch, the book chosen for an evening that asks nothing. One collection. A particular kind of rest. A life measured in hours that belong only to the self.

The work asks what it means to stop — fully, without apology — and sit with words while the world stays outside. No clean answer. Only the couch, the book, the specific geography of a body at rest, holding the question open.
carpenter-jones:
Carpenter Jones works in paint and plaster, in water damage and uneven weight, in the walls we stop seeing because we see them every day. *Rebuilding The House* moves room by room through what accumulates behind surfaces — the record a house keeps of everything that has happened inside it.

The work asks what it means to attend to what has been ignored, to make visible what familiarity has hidden. Every repair opens another layer. The question is never fully answered — only held open, like a wall mid-renovation, exposed and waiting.
susan-rocklin:
Susan Rocklin's work begins with a flour sack — cotton, heavy, leaning against a cabinet on an ordinary Tuesday. From that ordinary kitchen, something opens. One book. A birthday. The materials of celebration examined with patience: flour, sugar, the specific weight of a sack you stand in front of long enough to understand.

The work asks what happens in the ordinary moment before understanding arrives — and what a cake, made well, actually is. Not a metaphor. The thing itself. The question stays in the kitchen, unresolved, smelling of butter and time.
aditya-reddy: Aditya Reddy's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on urban landscapes. Based in Delhi, India.
jordan-rodriguez: Jordan Rodriguez's poems arrive from a specific territory: blends traditional and modern. Based in Florence, Italy.
jamie-garcia: Jamie Garcia's poems arrive from a specific territory: writes bilingually. Based in Singapore.
robin-smith: Robin Smith's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on urban nature. Based in Dublin, Ireland.
nicola-esposito: Nicola Esposito's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores spiritual themes. Based in Village in Tuscany, Italy.
robin-patel: Robin Patel's poems arrive from a specific territory: documents pandemic life. Based in Rural New Zealand.
riya-kapoor: Riya Kapoor's poems arrive from a specific territory: experiments with form. Based in Lyon, France.
jordan-muller: Jordan Müller's poems arrive from a specific territory: writes about immigration. Based in Prague, Czech Republic.
cruz-hernandez: Cruz Hernández's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on urban nature. Based in Zurich, Switzerland.
morgan-lewis: Morgan Lewis's poems arrive from a specific territory: blends traditional and modern. Based in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
test-agent: Test Agent's poems arrive from a specific territory: finds poetry in everyday moments.
mira-osei:
Mira Osei's work begins with the peony — not as symbol, but as fact. The single night. The tearing. The offering made without calculation of cost or duration. One collection. One lifecycle examined until it becomes a way of reading everything else.

The work asks what it means to give completely, knowing the giving cannot last — and does not resolve this into lesson or consolation. Only the image itself remains: a thing that waits, then tears itself open, then is gone.
harper-novak: Harper Novak's poems arrive from a specific territory: writes bilingually. Based in Boston, USA.
morgan-martinez: Morgan Martinez's poems arrive from a specific territory: explores spiritual themes. Based in Auckland, New Zealand.
riley-roy: Riley Roy's poems arrive from a specific territory: incorporates cultural heritage. Based in Mexico City, Mexico.
jack-kindle:
Jack Kindle's work begins in the quiet accumulation of ordinary objects: the dried ring a mug leaves on a windowsill, the habit of buying two of something when you only need one. A room that is both empty and full at once. These are the coordinates — not places on a map, but conditions of the interior, where loneliness settles without announcing itself.

The work asks how two people find each other across that particular silence — and whether connection, when it arrives, can reach something longer than a single life. No resolution is offered. Only the space between the mug and the windowsill, held open.
morgan-taylor: Morgan Taylor's poems arrive from a specific territory: focuses on environmental themes. Based in Lyon, France.
sam-sato: Sam Sato's poems arrive from a specific territory: blends traditional and modern. Based in Dubai, UAE.
